articles.title: New York City Is Leading the U.S. in Apartment Construction. Yes, Really.
articles.narrative: New York City is experiencing a significant surge in apartment construction, leading the nation in multifamily units under construction in Q1 2026, despite its high building costs and low vacancy rates. This boom is largely attributed to city and state initiatives, including residential-friendly rezonings in areas like Gowanus and Long Island City, and tax incentives such as the 467-m tax break for conversions. While 2025 saw the highest number of housing units completed since 1965, experts suggest that sustained policy changes, including further aggressive rezonings and improved tax incentives beyond the current 485-x program, are crucial to address the city's long-term housing shortage and maintain this development momentum.

articles.title: Cresa Hires Robert Gutman to Lead Structured Finance for Data Center Platform
It’s Still Early Innings for AI, Crypto in Commercial Real Estate: Forum
articles.narrative: A recent forum hosted by Commercial Observer explored the nascent integration of AI and cryptocurrency within the commercial real estate sector. Grant Cardone of Cardone Capital discussed his hybrid investment model, which combines stable multifamily real estate returns with the volatility of Bitcoin, aiming to offer investors better, more stable returns than traditional REITs. Separately, a panel featuring experts from Lev, BXP, and Brookfield Asset Management emphasized a pragmatic approach to AI adoption, advocating for specialized, task-specific implementations to achieve cost and time efficiencies rather than broad, unspecialized deployments. The panelists highlighted the importance of establishing a unified data source and a robust framework to ensure AI systems learn and compound effectively over time.

companies_people.label: OTHER
companies_people.firm_name: Chen Institute for Global Real Estate at New York University’s Stern School of Business

articles.title: Kings Capital Files Plans to Convert FiDi’s 61 Gold Street Into 108 Apartments
articles.narrative: Kings Capital has filed new plans with the New York City Department of Buildings to convert the vacant 17-story office building at 61 Gold Street in Manhattan's Financial District into 108 residential units. The updated proposal includes 1,536 square feet of ground-floor retail space, a new lobby, and other amenities. This project is part of a broader trend of office-to-residential conversions in the Financial District.

data_points.address: 61 Gold Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Includes 1,536 square feet of ground-floor retail space, a new lobby, mail room, and package room.
data_points.project_notes: The building was previously a nursing facility and has been vacant since 2020. New York-Presbyterian Hospital's Royal Charter Properties acquired the building in 2014.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: New York-Presbyterian Hospital's Royal Charter Properties

articles.title: Bay Club Company Buys El Segundo, Calif., Property for $42M: Updated
articles.narrative: Bay Club Company has acquired the 5.4-acre campus housing its Bay Club El Segundo facility for $42 million, completing a long-term strategy with seller Continental Development Corporation. This transaction gives Bay Club full ownership of both the buildings and the underlying land at the property, solidifying its presence in the South Bay area as a premier athletic and recreation club destination. The company's CEO noted that controlling the real estate provides permanent control over their future and the foundation for continued investment in the club and community.

data_points.notable_features: Located within the 100-acre Continental Park master-planned business center.
data_points.project_notes: Bay Club previously acquired the 100,000-square-foot building in 2017 for $29 million, along with a long-term ground lease and an option to purchase the underlying land.

articles.title: Demand for Logistics, Manufacturing Space Drives Wichita’s Pipeline of Projects
articles.narrative: Wichita's industrial real estate market concluded 2025 with stable fundamentals, driven by strong demand for large-format logistics and manufacturing space. Despite a slight increase in overall vacancy to 9%, this was primarily due to smaller bay turnover and properties being marketed but not yet move-in ready. The vacancy rate for larger industrial buildings (over 100,000 square feet) remained tight at 2.8%, indicating a persistent shortage of modern bulk space. Asking rents averaged $6.07 per square foot triple net annually, holding steady due to robust interest in quality warehouse and manufacturing facilities. The market's strategic position as a logistics and manufacturing hub continues to attract both regional and national firms, with a healthy development pipeline addressing the demand for modern facilities.
articles.market_intelligence: The rise in overall industrial vacancy in Wichita is primarily attributed to smaller-bay space turnover and the inclusion of properties being actively marketed but not yet move-in ready. The vacancy rate for larger industrial buildings (over 100,000 square feet) was significantly lower at 2.8%, indicating a persistent shortage of modern bulk space. Asking rents averaged $6.07 per square foot triple net annually. Cap rates for stabilized industrial properties ranged from the mid-7% to low-8% range in Q4 2025.

articles.title: Berkadia Brokers Sale of 184-Unit Active Adult Complex in Magnolia, Texas
articles.narrative: Berkadia has brokered the sale of Alders Magnolia, a 184-unit active adult complex located approximately 40 miles northwest of Houston. Aspens Senior Living acquired the community from Capitol Seniors Housing. The property, built in 2021, offers a wide array of amenities designed for active adult residents.

data_points.notable_features: Amenities include a pool, spa, theater, fitness center, demonstration kitchen, coffee bar, private wine lockers, putting green, bocce ball court, and lounge.

articles.title: LifeCare Development Underway on 117-Unit Seniors Housing Project in College Station
articles.narrative: LifeCare Development has commenced construction on The Blake at College Station, a 117-unit seniors housing project in College Station, Texas. The community will feature a mix of assisted living and memory care units, complemented by a range of culinary and wellness amenities. Blake Management Group will oversee the property's operations upon its scheduled completion in 2028.

data_points.address: 2324 Harvey Mitchell Pkwy S, College Station, TX
data_points.notable_features: 73 assisted living units and 44 memory care units; includes culinary and wellness amenities.

articles.title: SkyWalker Buys 73,747 SF Office Building in North Fort Worth
articles.narrative: SkyWalker Property Partners has acquired Alliance Commerce Center, a 73,747-square-foot office building in North Fort Worth, from Group O. The property was 90 percent leased at the time of sale and is situated within the AllianceTexas development.

data_points.notable_features: Located within AllianceTexas.

articles.title: Cravey Real Estate Arranges Sale of 13,014 SF Industrial Building in Corpus Christi
articles.narrative: Cravey Real Estate Services has facilitated the sale of a 13,014-square-foot industrial building located at 3930 Bratton Road in Corpus Christi, TX. The property, which includes 1,000 square feet of office space, was sold by a family estate to Victory Investment Portfolio LLC.

data_points.notable_features: Includes 1,000 square feet of office space.

articles.title: A life sciences sector that’s finally stabilizing? That’s what JLL is predicting
articles.narrative: JLL's 2026 U.S. Lab Property Report indicates that the U.S. life sciences real estate sector is showing signs of stabilization, with tenant demand for laboratory space beginning to outpace new supply in key markets. This shift is attributed to improving biotech funding, stronger capital flows, and increased tenant activity, suggesting the market may be entering an early recovery phase after a prolonged downturn. However, the sector still faces a significant supply imbalance, particularly in older properties, and recovery is uneven across the country, with primary markets like Boston, San Francisco Bay Area, San Diego, and Raleigh-Durham outperforming secondary markets.

people.name: Travis McCready
people.title: head of life sciences, Americas markets, and chair of JLL’s global life sciences advisory board

people.name: Mark Bruso
people.title: senior director of Boston and national life sciences research

articles.title: What’s new in Wisconsin TIF: Key changes under Acts 173 and 235
articles.narrative: Wisconsin has enacted significant changes to its tax incremental financing (TIF) laws through Acts 173 and 235, aiming to stimulate residential development and affordable housing. Key reforms include expanding the TIF district life by up to two years for affordable housing initiatives and clarifying the definition of "newly platted residential development." Additionally, a new Residential TID type has been authorized, specifically designed for owner-occupied, single-family or two-family residences, with distinct requirements for lot sizes and home square footage. These new TIDs will operate on a "pay-go" structure, with developers providing upfront financing for infrastructure and receiving reimbursement from generated increment, and are subject to a new three percent valuation test.

articles.title: BELL Construction breaks ground on 230,000-square-foot adult detention center in Tennessee’s Williamson County
articles.narrative: BELL Construction has broken ground on the new Williamson County Adult Detention Center, a 230,000-square-foot facility in Williamson County, Tennessee. This project is designed to enhance justice services by providing a more secure and streamlined environment for both staff and inmates, featuring 596 beds, 18 housing pods, and comprehensive support facilities. The new center is a key part of the county's multi-phase initiative to modernize its justice and detention infrastructure amidst ongoing growth.

data_points.notable_features: Includes 18 housing pods, 260 prefabricated metal cells, and a 70,000-square-foot parking garage with 156 secure parking stalls. Features central intake, booking, court services, laundry, food service, medical/dental facilities, infirmary, mental health housing, and recreation areas.
data_points.project_notes: This is the second project to break ground as part of Williamson County's multi-phase effort to update county facilities.

articles.title: PACE Loan Group closes $11.6 million in financing Irving data center conversion
articles.narrative: PACE Loan Group has provided $11.6 million in C-PACE financing for the conversion of a vacant industrial-flex property in Irving, TX, into the Vesgro Data Center. The project, developed by Infrascale Systems, will transform the 113,406-square-foot building into a 3.0 MW colocation data center with approximately 45,000 square feet of raised-floor data hall space. The financing will fund significant upgrades to HVAC, lighting, electrical infrastructure, generators, and the building envelope, with completion anticipated by September 2026. The project leverages the site's substantial power allocation and strategic location to meet the growing demand for Tier IV data centers, a trend noted by the lender in the current market.

data_points.address: 6001 Campas Circle Drive West, Irving, TX
data_points.notable_features: Located on a 4.69-acre site with 8.5MW power allocation, adjacent to DFW Airport and the Beltline Transit Station, offering strong infrastructure networks including fiber routes and power transmission lines.
data_points.project_notes: Conversion of a vacant industrial-flex property into a 3.0 MW colocation data center, delivering approximately 45,000 square feet of raised-floor data hall space. The C-PACE financing will fund improvements to HVAC systems, lighting, electrical infrastructure, generators, and the building envelope.

articles.title: Red Oak Capital provides $11.10 million in financing for Dayton assisted living community
articles.narrative: Red Oak Capital Holdings, LLC provided $11.10 million in bridge financing for the acquisition and capital improvements of Cottages of Clayton, a 90-unit assisted living community in Dayton, Ohio. The loan, structured under Red Oak's Core-Plus Bridge Loan Program, has an 18-month initial term and a loan-to-stabilized value of 69.0%. The borrower, Clayton Realco, LLC, plans to use the funds to complete targeted capital improvements, achieve HUD compliance, and enhance operational efficiency through their existing management platform.

data_points.notable_features: Seven-building community with cottage-style layout, each residential building includes its own kitchen, laundry, and lounge areas. Units feature private bathrooms and 24-hour emergency response.
data_points.project_notes: Loan structured under Red Oak Capital's Core-Plus Bridge Loan Program with an 18-month initial term and 69.0% loan-to-stabilized value. Funds will be used for acquisition, capital improvements, and bringing the property into HUD compliance.

articles.title: City Realty goes after lender for alleged online personal disclosures
articles.narrative: Boston developers Fred Starikov, Stephen Whalen, and Matthew Zuker, co-founders of City Realty Group, have filed a lawsuit against Eastern Bank, alleging the bank disclosed their confidential personal financial information on a loan sale platform. The lawsuit claims that personal financial statements and other sensitive data were posted on DebtX, a portal for loan sales, when Eastern Bank attempted to sell a $29 million construction loan and a $2 million line of credit originally obtained from HarborOne Bank (later acquired by Eastern). The developers are seeking monetary damages and a preliminary injunction to compel the bank to remove their information from the platform.

articles.title: Waymo drives into $220M land buy outside Phoenix
articles.narrative: Waymo, the autonomous vehicle subsidiary of Alphabet, has acquired a sprawling 5,458-acre proving grounds site in Surprise, Arizona, for $220 million. The property, previously used by Chrysler and Apple for vehicle testing, will serve as a dedicated facility for Waymo to advance its self-driving technology. This significant investment underscores Waymo's deepening commitment to the Phoenix area, which has become a key hub for autonomous vehicle development and testing.

data_points.address: 211th Avenue and Dove Valley Road, Surprise, AZ
data_points.notable_features: Site includes several circular tracks and grid-like streets for simulating urban and high-speed driving conditions.
data_points.project_notes: The City of Surprise approved the transfer of a development agreement from the prior owner to Waymo. The site was previously used by Chrysler and Apple for vehicle testing.

articles.market_intelligence: The Phoenix area has been a premier autonomous vehicle testing ground since Waymo's early rider program launched in 2018. Toyota also operates an 11,000-acre proving grounds near Wittmann, which it upgraded with a $50 million investment last year and opened to other companies in 2021. BNSF Railway has a proposed $3.2 billion logistics park and intermodal facility nearby.

articles.title: NYC’s top deals: NJ firm sells Bronx office for $16M
articles.narrative: Arctrust sold a commercial building and two parking facilities in the Bronx for $16.3 million to Jackson HT. Roosevelt Development. The property spans approximately 30,000 square feet. Additionally, a commercial condo unit with attached parking in Long Island City's Vesta building was acquired by Radha Soami Society Beas-America for $11.8 million.

data_points.address: 2244-2248 Westchester Avenue, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Two-story commercial building with two parking facilities.

articles.title: PolicyPro: City quietly drops pied-à-terre tax rules, comptroller bullish on revenue
articles.narrative: New York City tax officials have released proposed rules for implementing the contentious pied-à-terre tax, which is set to take effect in July. The 17-page proposal outlines how the Department of Finance will distinguish primary residents, who are exempt, from part-time owners subject to the tax, primarily through annual reviews of tax returns and residency-based benefits. The rules also address properties held by LLCs, corporations, and partnerships, requiring full ownership of the property interest or co-op shares for exemption eligibility. The city comptroller's office has revised its revenue projections, now expecting the tax to meet or exceed its annual $500 million target, a significant increase from earlier estimates, based on a clearer understanding of the tax base which includes over 13,586 units.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: New York City Department of Finance

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings

articles.title: Judge tosses Koreins’ name-dropping 1 Penn Plaza lawsuit against Vornado
articles.narrative: A Manhattan judge dismissed a lawsuit filed by the Korein family against Vornado Realty Trust concerning the ground lease at 1 Penn Plaza. The Koreins sought court intervention to determine the fair market value of the 2.5 million-square-foot office tower, alleging they could not find a neutral appraiser due to Vornado's market influence. The court ruled that the dispute must be resolved according to the ground lease's contractual appraisal provisions, not by judicial valuation. This decision is a setback for the Koreins, who are also pursuing another case related to the ground lease itself.

data_points.address: 1 Penn Plaza, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: 57-story office tower, formerly known as One Penn Plaza, underwent a massive renovation.
data_points.project_notes: Ground lease renewal in June 2023 was to increase annual ground rent from $2.5 million based on land's fair market value.

articles.title: Housing Notes: Wall Street is adding more finance jobs to NYC than anywhere else
articles.narrative: This article argues that the narrative of New York City's decline is unfounded, presenting data to show the city's economic resilience and evolution. The author highlights that NYC has gained the most finance jobs nationally since 2019, significantly outpacing Miami, and emphasizes the substantial growth of the tech sector, which now employs more private-sector workers than Wall Street. The piece also notes a strong return-to-office trend, with Manhattan office visitation exceeding pre-pandemic levels, driven by both finance and tech sectors. This diversification and job growth, particularly among younger, high-wage earners, is expected to sustain strong demand in NYC's housing market.

articles.title: FARE Act generates thousands of complaints, tenant refunds in first year
articles.narrative: New York City's Fairness in Apartment Rental Expenses (FARE) Act, which shifted the responsibility for broker fees from tenants to landlords, has generated over 2,000 complaints and inquiries in its first year. The Department of Consumer and Worker Protection has issued 74 summonses for 100 violations, resulting in approximately $27,000 in penalties and $15,000 in tenant refunds. While supporters like Councilmember Chi Ossé highlight reduced upfront costs for renters, the real estate industry, represented by the Real Estate Board of New York, argues the law has led to tighter inventory, higher rents, and market confusion, and continues to challenge it in court. Researchers note that a lack of reliable data makes it difficult to fully assess the law's impact amidst broader housing market pressures.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: New York City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Real Estate Board of New York

articles.title: DR Horton chases south Miami-Dade supply in $17M on Homestead land buy
articles.narrative: D.R. Horton has acquired 97 residential lots across two parcels in Homestead, FL, for $16.9 million, continuing its strategy to secure developable land in south Miami-Dade. This acquisition, which closed at approximately $174,000 per lot, underscores the ongoing trend of homebuilders expanding into the region due to land scarcity and more favorable pricing compared to the county's urban core. The company had previously placed the site under contract in 2024 with a mortgage deposit.

data_points.property_name: Aspen Estates East and Aspen Estates West
data_points.project_notes: The site was under contract from M Aspen Homestead in 2024 through a $2.53 million mortgage deposit.

articles.title: Trilogy wins over $1M in suit over leaking “Frankenstein” roof at luxury auto club
articles.narrative: A Miami-Dade Circuit Court judge awarded Trilogy Real Estate Group $1.2 million in damages and other awards in a lawsuit against Link Construction Group, the general contractor for its luxury motorsports club. The litigation centered on extensive roof leaks and other defects at six auto lofts within The Concours Club, which significantly delayed its planned opening. The court found Link Construction Group in breach of contract and held it primarily responsible for the defects and associated losses, including liquidated damages for lost rent and the cost of re-roofing. The judge also determined that Trilogy was justified in retaining nearly $2.5 million from Link's contract balance to cover repair expenses.

data_points.notable_features: Luxury motorsports club with a 2-mile circuit and high-end amenities, including two-story auto lofts above garages for viewing the track.
data_points.project_notes: The project involved the construction of six auto lofts with two-story mezzanines. The general contractor was hired in 2021 under a guaranteed maximum price contract. The club's planned 2023 opening was delayed due to construction defects.

articles.title: Trump scores approval to keep helipad at Mar-a-Lago after presidency
articles.narrative: The town of Palm Beach has approved President Donald Trump's request to retain a helipad at his Mar-a-Lago estate beyond his presidency. The decision follows lobbying efforts by the estate's legal team and Trump's security, despite initial concerns from town officials regarding noise and potential overuse. The agreement restricts the helipad's future use to emergencies only, with significant fines for violations, aiming to balance security needs with community concerns.

data_points.address: 1100 S Ocean Blvd, Palm Beach, FL
data_points.notable_features: Helipad use restricted to emergencies only after the president's term, with a $15,000 fine for the first violation.
data_points.project_notes: The U.S. Secret Service requested the helipad be reinstalled last year and made permanent in April. The town council approved the request on Tuesday.

articles.title: Pompano Beach mansion sells for record $14M as high-end development takes hold
articles.narrative: An oceanfront mansion in Pompano Beach, FL, sold for a record-setting $13.8 million, marking the highest single-family home sale in the city's history. The 7,760-square-foot property was acquired by Seattle developer Harold Griffith and his wife, Joan Griffith, from an affiliate of developer Leathem Stearn. This transaction highlights Pompano Beach's emergence as a luxury destination, attracting high-end development and a new wave of wealth due to its relative affordability compared to nearby markets.

data_points.notable_features: Oceanfront access with lighthouse views, five bedrooms, six-and-a-half bathrooms, four-bay garage, movie theater, gym, and a pool with a water slide, rope swings, and swim-up bar.

articles.market_intelligence: The previous record for a single-family home sale in Pompano Beach was $10 million for a property at 2593 Southeast Eighth Street. The prior record-holder, sold in 2023, is now back on the market for $15 million. Pompano Beach has invested millions in infrastructure upgrades and public space revitalization.

articles.title: Greystone smacks Lowensteins with second Bronzeville foreclosure as CMBS collapse deepens to $54M
articles.narrative: Greystone, acting as special servicer for CMBS bondholders, has filed a foreclosure lawsuit against a 68-unit multifamily building in Chicago's Bronzeville neighborhood, owned by an entity controlled by Raphael and John Lowenstein. The lawsuit seeks to recover over $19.4 million, including significant default interest and fees, on an original $15.4 million loan. This action follows a rapid decline in the property's value, which appraised at $23.6 million in 2022 but fell to $15.6 million by March, alongside plummeting occupancy and mounting operating expenses. The property has also accumulated multiple city code violations and mechanics liens, indicating severe neglect and financial distress.

data_points.notable_features: Property appraised at $23.6 million in 2022, re-appraised at $15.6 million in March, indicating a 34% value loss.
data_points.project_notes: Original loan issued by Morgan Stanley Bank in May 2022. Total payoff amount sought is over $19.4 million due to contractual penalties, including $1.6 million in default interest and $1.6 million for yield maintenance prepayment premium. The property's debt service coverage ratio fell to 0.98 by September 2024. City of Chicago filed five building code violation liens between October 2022 and April. Mechanics liens were filed in May 2024 for unpaid rehab work.

articles.title: Tucker, Wingspan cash out with $133M Skokie refi
articles.narrative: A joint venture between Tucker Development and Wingspan Development Group has secured a $133 million refinancing for The Henry at Harms Woods, a 294-unit mixed-use apartment complex in Skokie, IL. The new first mortgage replaces the original $100 million construction debt, resulting in a $33 million cash-out for the developers. This refinancing underscores the strong demand for newly delivered multifamily supply in the suburban Chicago market, which has experienced a constrained development pipeline.

data_points.notable_features: Mixed-use complex with 245 luxury apartments, 49 rental townhomes, and approximately 8,000 square feet of retail, located less than a mile west of Westfield Old Orchard mall.
data_points.project_notes: The developers previously acquired the 10.5-acre site for $13.3 million in 2023 and demolished a 130,000-square-foot office building to make way for the multifamily project. Original construction financing in October 2023 included a $77.3 million senior mortgage from Kennedy Wilson and mezzanine financing from Nationwide Insurance.

articles.market_intelligence: Chicago's multifamily market is noted as one of the most supply-constrained in the country, leading to strong interest from capital sources for new developments.

articles.title: L3 Capital reportedly scooping up Palmolive Building retail space
articles.narrative: L3 Capital is reportedly acquiring the retail condominium portion of the Palmolive Building in Chicago's Gold Coast from Nuveen. The transaction includes the space currently occupied by Louis Vuitton and other retail tenants. Nuveen had been marketing the 51,795-square-foot retail space, which it acquired in 2012 for $90 million. This acquisition aligns with L3 Capital's strategy of transforming Gold Coast retail properties, as evidenced by their recent redevelopment of a former bank branch into a Skims store.

data_points.notable_features: The 37-story Art Deco tower, also known as the Playboy Building, features luxury residential units above the retail space.
data_points.project_notes: Nuveen previously acquired the retail space in 2012 for $90 million.

articles.market_intelligence: The Gold Coast retail market is shifting from exclusive luxury brands to more accessible luxury. The Magnificent Mile retail market is rebounding, with new tenants moving into North Michigan Avenue. The former John Hancock Center is being redeveloped into a Marriott International Edition-branded hotel, and Water Tower Place is being converted into office space.

articles.title: Bridge fund in place for delayed Cook County property tax bills
articles.narrative: Cook County Board President Toni Preckwinkle has announced a $300 million bridge loan fund to assist local governments impacted by delayed property tax bills. This initiative aims to provide short-term financial relief, particularly for suburban tax districts heavily reliant on property tax revenue, and will also consider disinvested communities. The ongoing delays, attributed to a computer system overhaul, pandemic effects, and individual office struggles, have forced some districts, especially schools and libraries, to incur significant costs by taking out their own loans or using reserves.

data_points.project_notes: Bridge loan program broadened to ensure more areas can qualify; applications open July 2026. Suburban tax districts relying on property taxes for 50% or more of revenue are eligible, with disinvested communities also considered. Previous delays were due to an overhaul of legacy computer systems, pandemic effects, and individual office struggles.

articles.title: Houston Astros tee up entertainment district with purchase of Salvation Army shelter
articles.narrative: An entity associated with the Houston Astros acquired a 0.6-acre property in Houston, TX, previously owned by the Salvation Army. This acquisition is part of the Major League Baseball franchise's ongoing efforts to develop a large-scale entertainment district across from its home stadium, Daikin Park. The planned district will feature retail, a hotel, and an open viewing area, aiming to create a vibrant, year-round destination similar to other successful sports-anchored developments.

data_points.notable_features: Located across the street from Daikin Park (formerly Minute Maid Park).
data_points.project_notes: The property was previously home to the Salvation Army's Center for Hope. The Astros announced plans for an entertainment district in 2023, with a goal to start construction by late 2024. The broader entertainment district plans include 60,000 square feet of retail, a 17-story, 300-key hotel, and an open area for game viewing.

articles.title: Texas built-to-rent investors remain wary of federal housing bill
articles.narrative: A federal housing bill aimed at improving affordability and curbing institutional investment in single-family homes has significantly impacted the built-to-rent (BTR) development pipeline in Texas. While recent revisions to the 21st Century Renewing Opportunity in the American Dream (ROAD) to Housing Act, which removed a provision requiring large investors to sell BTR homes to individuals within seven years, are seen as a positive step, investors remain hesitant. Developers indicate that capital deployment for new BTR projects is largely frozen, awaiting final legislative approval and clarity from the White House. This cautious approach is particularly evident in Texas, a leading state for BTR construction, where several projects with substantial investment have stalled.

articles.title: Judge halts Dallas City Hall vote, putting redevelopment plans on ice
articles.narrative: A Dallas County District Judge has temporarily halted a planned vote by the Dallas City Council regarding the relocation or redevelopment of the Dallas City Hall building. The ruling, prompted by a petition from several council members, found that the city violated the Texas Open Meetings Act by not providing sufficient public notice for the special meeting. This temporary restraining order prevents any vote on the future of the I.M. Pei-designed landmark for 14 days, impacting ongoing discussions about the building's obsolescence and potential redevelopment, including a previous proposal to offer the site to the Dallas Mavericks for a new stadium.

data_points.notable_features: Landmark I.M. Pei-designed brutalist structure.
data_points.project_notes: The city manager would enter binding contracts once given approval for redevelopment. The Dallas Mavericks had previously considered the City Hall site for a new arena but have committed to the former Valley View mall site.

articles.title: Embattled Silver Star files for Chapter 11 bankruptcy for second time in four years
articles.narrative: Silver Star Properties, a Houston-based REIT, has filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy for the second time in four years, citing $75 million in liabilities against $100 million in assets. The company, which attempted to pivot from office investments to self-storage, is facing defaults on four loans and a foreclosure on a self-storage facility. This latest filing follows a previous bankruptcy in September 2023, which involved the sale of its 35-property office portfolio to address $217 million in debt and a contentious legal battle with its former CEO over alleged mismanagement.

data_points.project_notes: Silver Star Properties previously filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in September 2023 to sell its 35-property office portfolio and address $217 million in debt. The company has $75 million in liabilities and $100 million in assets, with four loans in default and a $5.75 million promissory note on a foreclosure-bound self-storage facility.

articles.title: Freeway-fronting hillside in Visitacion Valley poised for mixed-income multifamily development
articles.narrative: A property owner in San Francisco's Visitacion Valley is planning a mixed-income multifamily development comprising 45 units across three buildings. The project, designed by Schaub Li Architects, will include 26 one-bedroom, 18 two-bedroom, and one three-bedroom units, with five designated as deed-restricted affordable housing. The development is projected to cost approximately $8.5 million for construction, excluding other development expenses, and will also provide parking for cars and bicycles.

data_points.address: 3275-3333 San Bruno Avenue, San Francisco, CA
data_points.notable_features: Located on a hillside overlooking U.S. Highway 101, with five units designated as deed-restricted below-market-rate housing.
data_points.project_notes: Plans involve merging six vacant parcels into a single half-acre parcel. Construction cost estimate does not include all development costs.

articles.market_intelligence: San Francisco is mandated by the state to plan for 82,069 new housing units by 2031. Over 20,000 approved units are stalled due to rising construction costs, federal tariffs, and material shortages. In the previous year, only 1,453 new units were completed, a significant drop from over 5,000 in 2020, and plans for only 2,541 new homes were filed.

articles.title: Housing on high: Mission District church-turned-condo up for sale
articles.narrative: A unique 5,300-square-foot condominium, converted from a former Christian Science church known as The Light House, is on the market in San Francisco's Mission District for $6.8 million. The three-bedroom, three-bathroom residence, which retains many original architectural features, is being sold by John Nichols, co-founder of 1-800-Contacts. The property is expected to attract empty nesters, artists, and founders, according to the listing agents.

data_points.notable_features: Features 30-foot ceilings, exposed brick and steel, original stained-glass windows, repurposed wood flooring, and shared access to a landscaped greenbelt. Located across the street from Mission Dolores Park.
data_points.project_notes: The building was a former Christian Science church converted into four condominiums by developer Siamak Akhavan, who purchased it in 2011. The seller, John Nichols, bought the unit in 2016 for $6.1 million and previously leased it for $20,000 per month.

articles.title: Lafayette to consider upzoning to meet state housing requirements after lawsuit
articles.narrative: The City of Lafayette has settled a lawsuit with the Housing Action Coalition, agreeing to consider upzoning 130 acres along Mt. Diablo Boulevard and add more land for future housing development to meet state housing requirements. The settlement, which also includes a payment of $120,080 in attorney fees, aims to avoid prolonged litigation and increase the city's development potential by hundreds of units. Lafayette is mandated to entitle 2,114 new housing units by 2031.

data_points.notable_features: Proposed upzoning along Mt. Diablo Boulevard to increase density from 60 to 65 units per acre in some areas, and from 35 to 45 units per acre in others.
data_points.project_notes: Settlement agreement requires the city to consider land-use changes by the end of 2026 through its discretionary review and public hearing process. The city is committed to entitling 2,114 new housing units by 2031.

articles.title: Jane Kim leads CA insurance chief race, boasts $30K in real estate donations
articles.narrative: Jane Kim, a former San Francisco Board of Supervisors member, is leading the primary race for California's state insurance commissioner, a position gaining prominence due to recent wildfire-related insurance shortfalls. Kim is proposing a single-payer, state-backed disaster insurance program, inspired by New Zealand's model, where homeowners would pay a levy to the state via their private insurers for disaster coverage. The state would then invest these funds in the bond market, using earnings to finance fire safety initiatives. This approach aims to stabilize insurance costs for homeowners and prevent insurers from exiting the California market or raising premiums due to increased wildfire risk.

articles.title: The Yield Is Real but So Is the Risk Premium You Are Being Asked to Accept
articles.narrative: This article analyzes the current landscape of U.S. high-yield bonds, noting their leading position in trailing one-year yields among major asset classes, with JNK posting 6.59%. While approximately half of major asset classes are generating positive real yields above the 3.8% consumer inflation rate, the author cautions investors to consider payout sustainability, price volatility, and total return expectations. Historically, high yields have often preceded strong total return periods, but the credit cycle and default rates are critical variables. The current modest risk premium of 160 basis points between high-yield bonds and 30-year Treasuries suggests a narrower margin of safety, as the market may not have fully priced in slowing growth, sticky inflation, and geopolitical impacts. The author recommends a barbell approach within fixed income, favoring investment-grade corporate credit and agency mortgage-backed securities, alongside a selective, quality-biased allocation to high yield, emphasizing BB-rated issues.

articles.title: Vornado Brings Redeveloped PENN 2 to 90% Leased
articles.narrative: Vornado Realty Trust has achieved 90% occupancy at its redeveloped PENN 2 office tower in New York City's Penn District, following two new full-floor lease agreements. Altana AI signed a 10-year lease for 62,309 square feet on the 21st floor, establishing its new headquarters, while Veeva committed to a 12-year lease for 62,223 square feet on the 11th floor. The successful leasing reflects Vornado's strategy to transform the legacy building into a modern, world-class office environment, leveraging its prime location above a major transportation hub.

data_points.notable_features: Located atop a major transportation hub in the Penn District campus.

people.name: Glen Weiss
people.title: EVP – office leasing and co-head of real estate

articles.title: Rockpoint Scores Mid-Rise Apartment Property in Westwood
articles.narrative: Rockpoint, a Boston-based real estate private equity firm, has acquired Wilshire Margot, a 97-unit multifamily property in Los Angeles's Westwood neighborhood. The mid-rise building, constructed in 2007, is strategically located along the Wilshire corridor, offering residents convenient access to major employment centers like Century City and Beverly Hills, as well as UCLA. Rockpoint plans to implement a repositioning strategy to enhance the property's value, capitalizing on the area's strong demographics and limited multifamily supply.

data_points.notable_features: Mid-rise, type-I apartment building located along the Wilshire corridor, benefiting from proximity to major employment hubs and UCLA.

articles.market_intelligence: West Los Angeles is identified as one of the most supply-constrained multifamily markets in the country, characterized by strong demographics, proximity to employment centers, and a deep base of affluent renters.

articles.title: Marcus & Millichap Closes Deal for Bronx Industrial with Redevelopment Potential
articles.narrative: Marcus & Millichap has brokered the sale of an industrial property in the Bronx for $10.275 million. The asset, which includes a warehouse on a substantial lot, was acquired by Realterm. The transaction highlights the strong demand for industrial outdoor storage and last-mile logistics facilities in New York City, with investors willing to pay a premium for scarce vacant industrial land that offers redevelopment potential.

data_points.notable_features: Located in the Special Hunts Point District and a Qualified Opportunity Zone, delivered vacant for redevelopment potential.

articles.title: NAI Hiffman Achieves Full Occupancy at 137K-SF O’Hare Office Building
articles.narrative: NAI Hiffman has successfully brought the 136,973-square-foot office building at 5500 Pearl Street in Rosemont, Illinois, to 100% occupancy with a new 2,748-square-foot lease signed by Horn-McGowan Insurance Agency. This achievement marks the culmination of a multi-year repositioning strategy by owner Integris Ventures and NAI Hiffman, which acquired the property in 2018 with only 47.7% occupancy. The comprehensive upgrades, including new amenities and common area improvements, significantly boosted leasing activity and tenant interest.

data_points.project_notes: The 136,973-square-foot property was acquired in 2018 as a value-add investment with 47.7% occupancy. NAI Hiffman was engaged in 2019 to oversee repositioning efforts.

articles.title: Fun Is The New Foot Traffic As Trampoline Parks, Challenge Rooms Plot Huge Expansion
articles.narrative: Family entertainment concepts, including trampoline parks and challenge rooms, are demonstrating significant resilience and expansion in the retail real estate sector, driven by consumer demand for affordable local outings over costly theme park vacations. This trend is creating a reliable demand pipeline for brick-and-mortar retail, particularly for backfilling large-format vacancies. Operators and landlords view these entertainment venues as new anchors that generate foot traffic and serve as core drivers for retail strategy, especially in suburban areas and the Sun Belt. The sector's growth is also fueled by investors seeking to reposition empty mall anchors and second-generation retail boxes, transforming disused spaces into vibrant community hubs.

people.name: James Cook
people.title: Senior Director of Americas Retail Research

articles.title: Property Owners' Efforts To Green Their Buildings Are Hitting A Wall
articles.narrative: Commercial property owners are encountering significant obstacles in their efforts to further green their buildings, despite prior progress driven by local regulations, tenant demand, and lender requirements. Industry experts note that physical building constraints, the continued reliance on fossil fuels by the national power grid, and recent federal policy changes impacting renewable energy incentives are collectively hindering landlords' ability to reduce emissions. This stagnation in sustainability efforts could lead to a devaluation of assets, as green improvements have historically commanded a value premium. While initial steps like LED conversions and insulation have been widely adopted, more substantial retrofits involving core infrastructure changes are proving challenging due to space limitations and the carbon intensity of the broader energy supply.

people.name: Daren Moss
people.title: Head of Environmental, Social and Corporate Governance

people.name: Randy Topp
people.title: Senior Vice President of Energy Services and National Operations Support

articles.title: Spirit Airlines' South Florida HQ Scheduled For July Auction
articles.narrative: The former South Florida headquarters of Spirit Airlines, a four-building campus known as Spirit Central, is scheduled for auction in July following the airline's bankruptcy filing in late 2024. The 8.5-acre complex in Dania Beach, FL, which cost $275 million to develop and opened in 2024, includes a 180,000 SF office building, an amenity building, a training facility, and housing for visiting workers. Potential buyers can bid on the entire campus or individual portions, with Broward County commissioners reportedly considering a bid for the office building, parking garage, and training facility for up to $100 million to replace their current downtown Fort Lauderdale office.

data_points.notable_features: Four-building campus including an office building, amenity building, in-flight and flight operations training facility, and housing for visiting workers.
data_points.project_notes: The campus was developed by Spirit Airlines for $275 million and opened in 2024, seven months before the company filed for bankruptcy. The Broward County property appraiser valued the office building and land at $133 million.

articles.title: Owner Wins $1.3B Award In Dispute Over Foreclosed OC Properties
articles.narrative: An arbitration judge awarded Orange County property owner Mohammad Honarkar $1.34 billion in a dispute stemming from a fraudulently formed joint venture. The joint venture, led by Mahender Makhijani and Continuum Analytics, took control of 20 commercial properties in Laguna Beach and Orange County, including the Hotel Laguna. The award found Makhijani and his affiliates liable for fraudulent inducement, breach of contract, and forcible entry, after they allegedly used financing secured by Honarkar's properties to make a substantial portion of their promised $30 million equity contribution.

data_points.notable_features: Portfolio includes the Hotel Laguna and a quartet of retail and commercial buildings at Laguna Canyon Road near the Sawdust Festival grounds.
data_points.project_notes: The dispute involved a portfolio of 20 properties valued at $483.6 million. 14 of the 21 properties in the portfolio have been foreclosed on, and three are currently in foreclosure proceedings and under receivership. Honarkar was attempting to recover from pandemic impacts and a complex divorce when the joint venture was formed in 2021.

articles.title: The Scion Group Continues Student Housing Spree With $1.5B Acquisition
articles.narrative: The Scion Group, the largest owner of student housing in the U.S., is set to acquire Atlanta-based Student Quarters for approximately $1.5 billion. This transaction will add 13,000 beds across 21 markets to Scion's portfolio, bringing its total to nearly 118,000 beds across 190 properties. This acquisition marks Scion's second significant expansion in recent months, following a $910 million deal for a 7,578-bed portfolio in May, and is part of a broader strategy to expand its national footprint and capital deployment, which has exceeded $5 billion over the last two years.

data_points.project_notes: The acquisition will expand Scion's presence into five new locations, bringing its total to 94 university markets. The deal is expected to close in Q3 2026.

articles.market_intelligence: Student housing rents grew 1.2% year-over-year in Q1 2026 after modest declines since 2023. Spring 2026 enrollment was up 1% from the prior year to 18.6 million students. Mapletree Investments is planning to sell its 14,000-bed student housing portfolio in the U.S. and UK.
